summary: Burgundy is the benchmark for terroir-driven Pinot Noir (reds) and Chardonnay (whites), with Aligoté and Gamay as secondary grapes. Styles range from steely, mineral Chablis in the north through the profound reds of the Côte de Nuits and the world's greatest white wines of the Côte de Beaune, to the value-driven Côte Chalonnaise and the broad, fruity whites of the Mâconnais. Wines are defined more by precise vineyard origin (climat) than by producer.
classification: A four-tier pyramid based on vineyard site: Régional (e.g. Bourgogne, Mâcon); Village (named after the commune, e.g. Gevrey-Chambertin, Meursault); Premier Cru (named climats within a village); and Grand Cru at the apex (33 climats, each its own appellation, e.g. Chambertin, Montrachet). Chablis follows the same hierarchy with one Grand Cru (seven climats) and many Premiers Crus.

producers.name: Domaine de la Romanée-Conti (DRC)
producers.note: Monopole owner of Romanée-Conti and La Tâche; Burgundy's most legendary estate

summary: Beaujolais is dominated by the Gamay grape, producing bright, juicy, low-tannin reds often made by semi-carbonic maceration on granite soils. Quality spans simple Beaujolais Nouveau and Beaujolais-Villages up to the structured, age-worthy wines of the ten northern Crus, the best of which rival Burgundy in complexity.
classification: Twelve AOCs in three tiers: regional Beaujolais AOC; Beaujolais-Villages (from 38 northern communes); and the ten Crus du Beaujolais, the named-village appellations that sit at the top and never carry the word 'Beaujolais' on the label.

summary: A small, distinctive region between Burgundy and Switzerland working five grapes: whites Savagnin and Chardonnay, and reds Poulsard (Ploussard), Trousseau and Pinot Noir. It is famous for oxidative, sherry-like wines made under a veil of yeast (sous voile), above all the intense, nutty Vin Jaune, plus sweet Vin de Paille and the fortified Macvin.
classification: Four geographic AOCs (Arbois, Château-Chalon, L'Étoile, Côtes du Jura) plus three product appellations covering the whole region (Crémant du Jura, Macvin du Jura, Marc du Jura). Château-Chalon is a Vin Jaune-only appellation; Côtes du Jura is the large catch-all within which the smaller AOCs sit.

summary: An alpine region of fresh, light, high-acid wines, mostly white and built on the local Jacquère grape, with the aromatic Altesse (Roussette), rich Bergeron (Roussanne) and reds from Mondeuse, Gamay and Pinot Noir. Wines are typically crisp, mineral and mountain-fresh, with Chignin-Bergeron and Mondeuse offering the most depth and ageing potential.
classification: Three AOPs: Vin de Savoie (the broad appellation, which can append one of 16 named crus such as Apremont or Chignin), Roussette de Savoie (Altesse-only whites, with four crus), and Seyssel (still and sparkling). The named crus carry stricter yield and ripeness rules than the base appellation.
